description_en: Katsushige (克栄) places 'overcome' with 'flourish'. Represents an indomitable soul who naturally conquers all obstacles to build lasting, glorious success.
Katsutoshi (一敏) uses 'one' and 'clever/agile' (read as Toshi). Indicates an incredibly swift, remarkably sharp intellect that sets the boy apart.
Katsutoshi (一利) pairs 'one' with 'profit/advantage'. Highly practical and focused, expecting the child to bring distinct worth and capability to their work.

meaning_en: A genius adding to the haven.
description_en: Katsutoshi (加津俊) applies classic 'Katsu' phonetics with 'genius/sagacious'. Very sophisticated, grounding pure brilliance in deeply traditional imagery.
Katsunobu (一誠) uses the 'Katsu' reading for 'one' alongside 'sincerity/truth'. Highlights an uncompromising, extraordinarily honest personal code.
Katsunobu (一暢) blends 'one' with 'stretch/fluent' (Nobu). Suggests an open-hearted, highly adaptable person who easily expresses their true feelings.
Katsunobu (活伸) uses 'lively/vital' and 'expand'. Extremely active and developmental, envisioning a child whose life energy constantly reaches outwards.
Katsuhiko (佳津彦) combines 'excellent/beautiful', 'haven (Tsu)', and 'boy/prince'. Tremendously elegant, giving the classic 'Hiko' name a highly refined phonetic lift.
Katsuhiko (加津彦) uses standard phonetics 'Add-Haven' with 'boy'. Soft, deeply traditional, and historically very comforting and balanced.
Katsuhiko (嘉津彦) leverages 'auspicious/praise' (Ka). Deeply celebratory; the parents' profound joy is permanently embedded into this classic sounding name.
Katsuhisa (活寿) places 'lively/living' with 'longevity'. Represents a remarkably active, eternally youthful person living a vibrantly long life.
Katsuhisa (且久) merges the literary 'moreover' with 'long time'. Imparts a distinctly intellectual, highly traditional feel of unbroken endurance.
Katsuhisa (功久) combines 'achievement/merit' with 'long time'. Highlights an incredibly diligent worker whose notable successes stand the test of time.
Katsuhiro (一宏) pairs 'one' (read Katsu) with 'vast/macroscopic'. Suggests a deeply foundational, universally expansive intellect or presence.
Katsuhiro (一広) uses 'one' and 'broad/spacious'. Emphasizes an extremely generous, massively open heart that welcomes everyone.
Katsuhiro (一裕) matches 'one' with 'abundant/plentiful'. Cultivates an atmosphere of absolute security, deep wealth, and relaxed, steady living.
Katsumasa (且将) combines the classical 'moreover' with 'commander/general'. Exceedingly authoritative and antique, fitted for a highly strategic, respected leader.
Katsumasa (且昌) pairs 'moreover' with 'prosperous/bright' (Masa). An incredibly sunny, deeply traditional name promising continuous elevation in life.
Katsumasa (且政) utilizes 'moreover' and 'government/rule'. Profoundly serious and civic-minded, aiming for a life of high public responsibility.
Katsuya (一矢) means 'one arrow' or 'to shoot a single arrow'. Intensely sharp, incredibly focused, and undeniably cool contextually.
Katsuya (加津也) applies soft, traditional phonetics ending with the stylish 'ya' (也). Exceptionally balanced between vintage charm and modern rhythm.
Katsuya (夏津也) starts with 'summer'. Gives the otherwise classic-sounding phonetic arrangement an extremely bright, warm, and highly memorable seasonal twist.
Kanta (勘太) couples 'intuition/perception' with the bold 'thick/big' suffix. Indicates an extremely sharp, street-smart, heavily grounded youth.
Kanta (勧太) utilizes 'encourage/recommend' and 'big'. Suggests a highly supportive, tremendously motivating individual who naturally propels others forward.
Kanta (寛太) marries 'tolerant/generous' with 'thick/big'. Extremely gentle yet physically and mentally imposing; a big-hearted giant.
Kichi (葵一) blends 'hollyhock' (associated with nobility) and 'one'. Strikingly unusual for 'Ki-ichi', imparting severe elegance and deep lineage.
Kichi (嘉一) uses 'auspicious/praise' and 'one'. Powerfully celebratory, confirming the boy as an exceedingly joyous firstborn blessing.
Kichi (喜一) pairs 'joy' with 'one'. An incredibly classic, universally understood expression of immense, unadulterated happiness at birth.

meaning_en: A cheerful boy of many joys.
description_en: Kitarou (喜多朗) combines 'joy', 'many', and 'cheerful'. Unbelievably sunny and optimistic, foreseeing a life flooded with smiles and clear skies.
Kitarou (喜太郎) uses 'joy', 'plump/big', and 'son'. Deeply classic and inherently happy, it is the quintessential name for an immensely cherished cheerful boy.
Kitarou (希太郎) replaces 'joy' with 'hope/rare'. Elevates the name's meaning to focus deeply on brilliant futures and extraordinary potential.
Kippei (輝平) pairs 'radiance/shine' with 'peace'. Exceptionally assuring, depicting a deeply calm life that nevertheless shines entirely bright.
Kippei (吉平) uses 'good luck/joy' and 'peace'. Incredibly standard and completely trusted; a historically proven conduit for good fortune.
Kimiaki (貴美昭) utilizes dense, three-kanji phonetics ('noble-beauty-bright'). Extravagantly elegant, sounding more akin to high nobility or artistic pedigree.
Kimiaki (君晃) applies the respectful 'Kimi' alongside 'radiant/shining'. Highly dignified, wishing for the boy's reputation to shine brightly everywhere.
Kimiaki (君明) uses 'Kimi' and 'bright/clear' (Mei/Aki). Denotes absolute honesty, clarity of intent, and a naturally noble, beloved character.
Kimio (王夫) pairs 'king'/'ruler' (read interestingly as Kimi) with 'husband/man'. Extraordinarily bold, casting an intensely commanding and elite aura.
Kimio (喜三男) employs 'joy-three-man'. Exceptionally literal for a deeply loved third son, wrapped in very standard, trustworthy phonetics.
Kimio (喜三夫) alters the ending to 'husband/man' (夫). Slightly softer than '男' but maintains the exact historical, deeply grateful intent.
Kiminobu (公延) matches 'public/prince' (Kimi) with 'prolong/stretch'. Greatly refined, indicating a highly distinguished life stretching elegantly into old age.
Kiminobu (公伸) uses 'public/prince' and 'expand'. Extremely active conceptually, representing an expansive, continually growing influence from a highly cultured base.
Kiminobu (公信) pairs 'public/prince' with 'trust'. A highly ethical, deeply principled name denoting someone completely trusted by society.
Kimiharu (君治) blends 'Kimi' with 'rule/heal' (Haru). Depicts an incredibly kind yet deeply capable leader dedicated to bringing order and peace.
Kimiharu (君春) matches 'Kimi' with 'spring'. Overwhelmingly gentle and bright, recalling an elegant youth bringing warmth after a long winter.
Kimiharu (公治) uses 'public/prince' and 'govern/heal'. Strongly civic-minded, indicating immense political skill and a highly polished sense of justice.
Kyouta (杏太) uses 'apricot' (often read 'An', here 'Kyou') and 'big'. Very fresh and vividly botanical, an excellent mix of natural charm and sturdy masculinity.
Kyouta (挙大) literally means 'raise up big'. Immensely energetic, denoting a child who acts with massive enthusiasm and continuously elevates himself.
Kyouta (亨太) brings 'go smoothly' alongside the solid 'thick/big'. Deeply promising, intending that every big endeavor in the boy's life proceeds without friction.

meaning_en: Joy of a generation/era, number one.
description_en: Kiyokazu (喜代一) uses 'joy-generation-one'. Unyielding in its classicism; intensely joyful and historically profound, celebrating a crucial first son.
Kiyokazu (喜代数) replaces 'one' with 'number/count'. Imparts an exceptionally archaic, slightly poetic wish for uncountable generations of continued happiness.
